parentQuestion: A patient in diabetic ketoacidosis presents with rapid, deep breathing (Kussmaul...

scaffolds.q: What is the relationship between CO₂ and blood acidity?
options: CO₂ has no effect on pH
scaffolds.explain: More CO₂ means MORE acidic blood. here's the chemistry: CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid) ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. When CO₂ increases, this equilibrium shifts RIGHT, producing more H⁺ (which LOWERS pH = more acidic). When CO₂ decreases (hyperventilation), the equilibrium shifts LEFT, reducing H⁺ (pH rises = less acidic). Open yourself to this:this: every breath you take affects your blood pH. Breathing IS acid-base regulation.

Acidosis means blood pH BELOW normal (< 7.35) — too acidic, too many H⁺ ions. Hold this thought:: normal blood pH is 7.35-7.45 (slightly alkaline). Acidosis disrupts enzyme function, protein structure, and cellular processes. Below 6.8? Incompatible with life. Surrender to the logic here:the narrow margin between health and crisis. pH regulation is CRITICAL.
